hama: In church affairs, a vessel to contain wine for the eucharist; also, a wine measure, as a Cask, a pipe.
hammite: A sand-stone or free-stone, of a pale brown color, very heavy, of a lax texture, composed of small round granules, cemented by an earthy sparry matter. The grit or granules are small stalagmites, composed of crusts or coats including one another. It is the roe- stone or oolite of recent authors.
harquebuse: A hand gun; a species of fire arms, anciently used, which was cocked with a wheel. It carried a ball that weighed nearly two ounces. A larger kind, used in fortresses carried a ball of three ounces and a half.
huguenot: A name formerly given to a protestant in France.
huguenotisim: The religion of the Huguenots in France.
hulk: The body of a ship, or decked vessel of any kind; but the word is applied only to the body of an old ship or vessel which is laid by as unfit for service. A sheer-hulk is an old ship fitted with an apparatus to fix or take out the masts of a ship.
To take out the entrails; as, to hulk a hare.
hull: The outer covering of any thing, particularly of a nut or of grain. Johnson says, the hull of a nut covers the shell.

The frame or body of a ship, exclusive of her masts, yards and rigging. To lie a hull, in seamen’s language, is to lie as a ship without any sail upon her, and her helm lashed a-lee. To strike a hull, in a storm, is to take in the sails, and lash the helm on the lee-side of a ship.
To strip off or separate the hull or hulls; as, to hull grain.
To pierce the hull of a ship with a cannon-ball.
To float or drive on the water without sails.
hulotheism: The doctrine or belief that matter is God, or that there is no God, except matter and the universe.
hum: To utter the sound of bees; to buzz.
To make an inarticulate buzzing sound. The cloudy messenger turns me his back, And hums-- .
To pause in speaking, and make an audible noise like the humming bees. He hummed and hawed.' .
To make a dull, heavy noise like a drone. Still humming, on their drowsy course they took.
human: Belonging to man or mankind; pertaining or relating to the race of man; as a human voice; human shape; human nature; human knowledge; human life.
Having the qualities of a man.
Profane; not sacred or divine; as a human author.
humane: Having the feelings and dispositions proper to man; having tenderness, compassion, and a disposition to treat others with kindness; particularly in relieving them when in distress, or in captivity, when they are helpless or defenseless; kind; benevolent.
Inclined to treat the lower orders of animals’ with tenderness.
humanely: With kindness, tenderness or compassion; as, the prisoners were treated humanely.
Ina humane manner; with kind feelings.
humanist: A professor of grammar and rhetoric; a philologist; a term used in the universities of Scotland.
One versed in the knowledge of human nature.
humanity: The peculiar nature of man, by which he is distinguished from other beings. Thus Christ, by his incarnation, was invested with humanity.
Mankind collectively; the human race. If he is able to untie those knots, he is able to teach alll humanity. It is a debt we owe to humanity.

The kind feelings, dispositions and sympathies of man, by which he is distinguished from the lower orders of animals; kindness; benevolence; especially, a disposition to relieve persons in distress, and to treat with tenderness those who are helpless and defenseless; opposed to cruelty.
A disposition to treat the lower orders of animals with tenderness, or at least to give them no unnecessary pain.
The exercise of kindness; acts of tenderness.
humanize: To soften; to render humane; to subdue dispositions to cruelty, and render susceptible of kind feelings. Was it the business of magic to humanize our natures?
humankind: The race of man; mankind; the human species.
humanly: After the manner of men; according to the opinions or knowledge of men. The present prospects, humanly speaking, promise a happy issue.
humbird: A very small bird of the genus Trochilus; so called from the sound of its wings in flight. The rostrum is subulate, filiform, and longer than the head; the tongue is filiform and tubulous. It never lights to take food, but feeds while on the wing.
humming-bird: A very small bird of the genus Trochilus; so called from the sound of its wings in flight. The rostrum is subulate, filiform, and longer than the head; the tongue is filiform and tubulous. It never lights to take food, but feeds while on the wing.
humble: Low; opposed to high or lofty. Thy humble nest built on the ground.
Low; opposed to lofty or great; mean; not magnificent; as a humble cottage. A humble roof, and an obscure retreat.

Lowly; modest; meek; submissive; opposed to proud, haughty, arrogant or assuming. In an evangelical sense, having a low opinion of one’s self, and a deep sense of unworthiness in the sight of God. God resisteth the proud, but giveth grace to the humble. James 4:6. Without a humble imitation of the divine author of our blessed religion, we can never hope to be a happy nation.
To abase; to reduce to a low state. This victory humbled the pride of Rome. The power of Rome was humbled, but not subdued.
To crush; to break; to subdue. The battle of Waterloo humbled the power of Buonaparte.
humblebee: A bee of a large species, that draws its food chiefly from clover flowers.
humbled: Made low; abased; rendered meek and submissive; penitent.
humbleness: The state of being humble or low; humility; meekness.
herpes: Tetters; an eruption on the skin; erysipelas; ringwormThis disease takes various names according to its form or the part affected. A term applied to several cutaneous eruptions, from their tendency to spread or creep from one part of the skin to another. An eruption of vesicles in small distinct clusters, accompanied with itching or tingling; including the shingles, ringworm.
herpetic: Pertaining to the herpes or cutaneous eruptions; resembling the herpes, or partaking of its nature; as herpetic eruptions.
herpetologist: A person versed in herpetology, or the natural history of reptiles.
herpetology: A description of reptiles; the natural history of reptiles, including oviparous quadrupeds, as the crocodile, frog and tortoise, and serpents. The history of the latter is called ophiology.
herring: A fish of the genus Clupea. Herrings, when they migrate, move in vast shoals, and it is said that the name is formed from the Teutonic here, heer, an army or multitude. They come from high northern latitudes in the spring, and visit the shores of Europe and America, where they are taken and salted in great quantities.
herring-fishery: The fishing for herrings, which constitutes an important branch of business with the English, Dutch and Americans.
hers: hurzpossessive; as, this house is hers, that is, this is the house of her. But perhaps it would be more correct to consider hers as a substitute for the noun and adjective, in the nominative case. Of the two houses, hers is the best, that is, her house is the best.
herschel: her’shel. A planet discovered by Dr. Herschel, in 1781.
herse: In fortification, a lattice or portcullis in the form of a harrow, set with iron spikes. It is hung by a rope fastened to a moulinet, and when a gate is broken, it is let down to obstruct the passage. It is called also a sarrasin or cataract, and when it consists of straight stakes without cross-pieces, it is called orgues. Herse is also a harrow, used for a chevaux de frise, and laid in the way or in breaches, with the points up, to obstruct or incommode the march of an enemy.
A carriage for bearing corpses to the grave. It is a frame only, or a box, as in England, borne on wheels.
A temporary monument set over a grave.
hers. To put on or in a herse.
herself: This denotes a female, the subject of discourse before mentioned, and is either in the nominative or objective case. In the nominative it usually follows she, and is added for the sake of emphasis, or emphatical distinction; as, she herself will bear the blame. The daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself. Exodus 2:5.
Having the command of herself; mistress of her rational powers, judgment or temper. The woman was deranged, but she is now herself again. She has come to herself.
In her true character; as, the woman acts like herself.
hersillon: In the military art, a plank or beam, whose sides are set with spikes or nails, to incommode and retard the march of an enemy.
hesitancy: A doubting; literally, a stopping of the mind; a pausing to consider; dubiousness; suspense. The reason of my hesitancy about the air is-- .
hesitant: Hesitating; pausing; wanting volubility of speech.
hesitate: To stop or pause respecting decision or action; to be doubtful as to fact, principle or determination; to be in suspense or uncertainty; as, he hesitated whether to accept the offer or not. We often hesitate what judgment to form. It is never transitive, unless by poetic license. Just hint a fault, and hesitate dislike.
To stammer; to stop in speaking.
hesitation: A pausing or delay in forming an opinion or commencing action; doubt; suspension of opinion or decision, from uncertainty what is proper to be decided. When evidence is clear, we may decide without hesitation.
A stopping in speech; intermission between words; stammering.
hesperian: An inhabitant of a western country.
heteroclite: In grammar, a word which is irregular or anomalous either in declension or conjugation, or which deviates from the ordinary forms of inflection in words of a like kind. It is particularly applied to nouns irregular in declension.
Any thing or person deviating from common forms.
Irregular; anomalous; deviating from ordinary forms or rules.
heteroclitic: Irregular; anomalous; deviating from ordinary forms or rules.
heteroclitical: Irregular; anomalous; deviating from ordinary forms or rules.
heteroclitous: Heteroclitic. HETERODOxHETERODOXyYHeresy; an opinion or doctrine contrary to the doctrines of the Scriptures, or contrary to those of an established church.
In theology, heretical; contrary to the faith and doctrines of the true church; or more precisely, contrary to the real doctrines of the Scriptures; as a heterodox opinion; opposed to orthodox.
heterogeneal: Of a different kind or nature; unlike or dissimilar in kind; opposed to homogeneous. The light whose rays are all alike refrangible, I call simple, homogeneal and similar; and that whose rays are some more refrangible than others, I call compound, heterogeneal and dissimilar. Heterogeneous nouns, are such as are of different genders in the singular and plural numbers; as hic locus, of the masculine gender in the singular, and hi loci and haec loca, both masculine and neuter in the plural. Hoc coelum, neuter in the singular; hi coeli, masculine in the plural. Heterogeneous quantities, are those which are of such different kind and consideration, that one of them, taken any number of times, never equals or exceeds’ the other. Heterogeneous surds, are such as have different radical signs.
heterogeneous: Of a different kind or nature; unlike or dissimilar in kind; opposed to homogeneous. The light whose rays are all alike refrangible, I call simple, homogeneal and similar; and that whose rays are some more refrangible than others, I call compound, heterogeneal and dissimilar. Heterogeneous nouns, are such as are of different genders in the singular and plural numbers; as hic locus, of the masculine gender in the singular, and hi loci and haec loca, both masculine and neuter in the plural. Hoc coelum, neuter in the singular; hi coeli, masculine in the plural. Heterogeneous quantities, are those which are of such different kind and consideration, that one of them, taken any number of times, never equals or exceeds’ the other. Heterogeneous surds, are such as have different radical signs.
heterogeneity: Opposition of nature; contrariety or dissimilitude of qualities. [lll
Dissimilar part; something of a different kind.
hosanna: s as z. An exclamation of praise to God, or an invocation of blessings. In the Hebrew ceremonies, it was a prayer rehearsed on the several days of the feast of tabernacles, in which this word was often repeated.
hose: hosen or hose; pron. hoze, ho’zn.
Stockings; coverings for the legs. This word, in mercantile use, is synonymous with stockings, though originally a very different garment.
A leathern pipe, used with fire-engines, for conveying water to extinguish fires.
hosier: ho’zhur. One who deals in stockings and socks
hospitable: Receiving and entertaining strangers, with kindness and without reward; kind to strangers and guests; disposed to treat guests with generous kindness; as a hospitable man.
Proceeding from or indicating kindness to guests; manifesting generosity; as a hospitable table; hospitable rites.
Inviting to strangers; offering kind reception; indicating hospitality. To where yon taper cheers the vale, With hospitable ray.
hospitably: With kindness to strangers or guests; with generous and liberal entertainment.
hospital: A building appropriated for the reception of sick, infirm and helpless paupers, who are supported and nursed by charity; also, a house for the reception of insane persons, whether paupers or not, or for seamen, soldiers, foundlingswho are supported by the public, or by private charity, or for infected persons.
A place for shelter or entertainment.
hospitality: The act or practice of receiving and entertaining strangers or guests without reward, or with kind and generous liberality. A bishop--must be given to hospitality. 7 Timothy 3:2. Hospitality I have found as universal as the face of man.
hospitaller: Properly, one residing in a hospital for the purpose of receiving the poor and strangers. The hospitallers were an order of knights who built a hospital at Jerusalem for pilgrims. They were called knights of St. John, and are the same as the knights of Malta.
hospitate: To reside or lodge under the roof of another.
host: One who entertains another at his own house, without reward. Homer never entertained guests or hosts with long speeches.
One who entertains another at his house for reward; an innkeeper; a landlord.
A guest; one who is entertained at the house of another. The innkeeper says of the traveler, he has a good host, and the traveler says of his landlord, he has a kind host.
An army; a number of men embodied for war.
hostage: A person delivered to an enemy or hostile power, as a pledge to secure the performance of the conditions of a treaty or stipulations of any kind, and on the performance of which the person is to be released.
hostess: A female host; a woman who entertains guests at her house.
hostess-ship: The character or business of a hostess.
hostile: Belonging to a public enemy; designating enmity, particularly public enmity, or a state of war; inimical; as a hostile band or army; a hostile force; hostile intentions.
Possessed by a public enemy; as a hostile country.
hostility: The state of war between nations or states; the actions of an open enemy; aggression; attacks of an enemy. These secret enmities broke out in hostilities. Hostility being thus suspended with France. We have carried on even our hostilities with humanity.
Private enmity; a sense less proper.
hostler: hos’ler. The person who has the care of horses at an inn.
hot: Having sensible heat; opposed to cold; as a hot stove or fire; a hot cloth; hot liquors. Hot expresses more than warm.
Ardent in temper; easily excited or exasperated; vehement. Achilles is impatient, hot and revengeful.
Violent; furious; as a hot engagement or assault.
Eager; animated;; brisk; keen; as a hot pursuit, or a person hot in a pursuit.
hotbed: In gardening, a bed of earth and horsedung or tanner’s bark, covered with glass to defend it form the cold air, intended for raising early plants, or for nourishing exotic plants of warm climates, which will not thrive in cool or temperate air.
hotbrained: Ardent in temper; violent; rash; precipitate; as hotbrained youth.
hotchpot: Properly, a mingled mass; a mixture of ingredients.

In law, a mixing of lands. Thus lands given in frank-marriage to one daughter, shall, after the death of the ancestor, be blended with the lands descending to her and then be divided in equal portions to all the daughters.
hotcockles: A play in which one covers his eyes, and guesses who strikes him, or his hand placed behind him.
hotel: An inn; a house for entertaining strangers or travelers. It was formerly a house for genteel strangers or lodgers, but the name is now given to any inn.
hotheaded: Of ardent passions; vehement; violent; rash.
hothouse: A house kept warm to shelter tender plants and shrubs from the cold air; a place in which the plants of warmer climates may be reared, and fruits ripened.
A bagnio, or place to sweat and cup in.
hotly: Ardently; vehemently; violently; as a stag hotly pursued.
hotmouthed: Headstrong; ungovernable. That hotmouthed beast that bears against the curb.
hotness: Sensible heat beyond a moderate degree or warmth. of the lower jaw.
ha: an exclamation, denoting surprise, joy or grief. With the first or long sound of a, it is used as a question, and is equivalent to “What do you say?” When repeated, ha, ha, it is an expression of laughter, or sometimes it is equivalent to “Well! it is so.”
haak: A fish. Habeas Corpus, A writ for delivering a person from false imprisonment, or for removing a person from one court to another.
haberdasher: A seller of small wares; a word little used or not at all in the U. States.
haberdashery: The goods and wares sold by a haberdasher.
habergeon: A coat of mail or armor to defend the neck and breast. It was formed of little iron rings united, and descended from the neck to the middle of the body.
habiliment: A garment; clothing; usually in the plural, habiliments, denoting garments, clothing or dress in general.
habit: Garb; dress; clothes or garments in general. The scenes are old, the habits are the same, We wore last year. There are among the statues, several of Venus, in different habits.
A coat worn by ladies over other garments.

State of any thing; implying some continuance or permanence; temperament or particular state of a body, formed by nature or induced by extraneous circumstances; as a costive or lax habit of body; a sanguine habit.

A disposition or condition of the mind or body acquired by custom or a frequent repetition of the same act. Habit is that which is held or retained, the effect of custom or frequent repetition. Hence we speak of good habits and bad habits. Frequent drinking of spirits leads to a habit of intemperance. We should endeavor to correct evil habits by a change of practice. A great point in the education of children, is to prevent the formation of bad habits. Habit of plants, the general form or appearance, or the conformity of plants of the same kind in structure and growth.
é. To dress; to clothe; to array. They habited themselves like rural deities.
habitable: That may be inhabited or dwelt in; capable of sustaining human beings; as the habitable world. Some climates are scarcely habitable.
habitably: In such a manner as to habitable.
habitant: An inhabitant; a dweller; a resident; one who has a permanent abode in a place.
habitation: Act of inhabiting; state of dwelling.

Place of abode; a settled dwelling; a mansion; a house or other place in which man or any animal dwells. The stars may be the habitations of numerous races of beings. The Lord blesseth the habitation of the just. Proverbs 3:33.
habited: Clothed; dressed. He was habited like a shepherd.
habitual: Formed or acquired by habit, frequent use or custom. Art is properly an habitual knowledge of certain rules and maxims.
Customary; according to habit; as the habitual practice of sin; the habitual exercise of holy affections. It is the distinguishing mark of habitual piety to be grateful for the most common blessings.
Formed by repeated impressions; rendered permanent by continued causes; as an habitual color of the skin.
habitually: By habit; customarily; by frequent practice or use; as habitually profane; habitually kind and benevolent.
habituate: To accustom; to make familiar by frequent use or practice. Men may habituate themselves to the taste of oil or tobacco. They habituate themselves to vice. Let us habituate ourselves and our children to the exercise of charity.
To settle as an inhabitant in a place.
habituating: Accustoming; making easy and familiar by practice.
habitude: Relation; respect; state with regard to something else.
Frequent intercourse; familiarity.é To write well, one must have frequent habitudes with the best company.'/ .
Customary manner or mode of life; repetition of the same acts; as the habitudes of fowls or insects.
habnab: At random; by chance; without order or rule.
hack: To cut irregularly and into small pieces; to notch; to mangle by repeated strokes of a cutting instrument.
To speak with stops or catches; to speak with hesitation.
A horse kept for hire; a horse much used in draught, or in hard service; any thing exposed to hire, or used in common.
A coach or other carriage kept for hire.
hacking: Chopping into small pieces; mangling; mauling.
hackle: To comb flax or hemp; to separate the coarse part of these substances from the fine, by drawing them through the teeth of a hatchel.
A hatchel. The latter word is used in the U. States.
Raw silk; any flimsy substance unspun.
A fly for angling, dressed with feathers or silk.
hackly: Rough; broken as if hacked. In mineralogy, having fine, short, and sharp points on the surface; as a hackly fracture.
hackmatack: The popular name of the red larch, the Pinus microcarpa.
hackney: A pad; a nag; a pony.
A horse kept for hire; a horse much used.
A coach or other carriage kept for hire, and often exposed in the streets of cities. The word is sometimes contracted to hack.
Any thing much used or used in common; a hireling; a prostitute.
Let out for hire; devoted to common use; as a hackney-coach.
hackney-coachman: A man who drives a hackney-coach.
hackneyed: Practiced; accustomed. He is long hackneyed in the ways of men.
hackneyman: A man who lets horses and carriages for hire.
hackster: A bully; a ruffian or assassin.
hacqueton: A stuffed jacket formerly worn under armor, sometimes made of leather.
had: and pp. of have; contracted from Sax. haefd, that is, haved; as, I had; I have had. In the phrase, “I had better go,” it is supposed that had is used for would; “I’d better go.” The sense of the phrase is, “it would be better for me to go.”
haddock: A fish of the genus Gadus or cod, and order of Jugulars. It has a long body, the upper part of a dusky brown color, and the belly of a silvery hue; the lateral line is black. This fish breeds in immense numbers in the northern seas, and constitutes a considerable article of food.
hade: Among miners, the steep descent of a shaft; also, the descent of a hill. In mining, the inclination or deviation from the vertical of a mineral vein.
haft: A handle; that part of an instrument or vessel which is taken into the hand, and by which it is held and used. It is used chiefly for the part of a sword or dagger by which it is held; the hilt.
hag: An ugly old woman; as an old hag of threescore.
A witch; a sorceress; an enchantress.
A cartilaginous fish, the Gastrobranchus, which enters other fishes and devours them. It is about five or six inches long, and resembles a small eel. It is allied to the lamprey.
Appearances of light and fire on horses’ manes or men’s hair, were formerly called hags.
hagard: Literally, having a ragged look, as if hacked or gashed. Hence, lean; meager; rough; having eyes sunk in their orbits; ugly.
Wild; fierce; intractable; as a hagard hawk.
hagardly: In a hagard or ugly manner; with deformity.
hagborn: Born of a hag or witch.
haggess: A mess of meat, generally pork, chopped and inclosed in a membrane.
haggle: To cut into small pieces; to notch or cut in an unskillful manner; to make rough by cutting; to mangle; as, a boy haggles a stick of wood. Suffolk first died, and York all haggled o’er, Comes to him where in gore he lay insteep’d.
To be difficult in bargaining; to hesitate and cavil.
haggled: Cut irregularly into notches; made rough by cutting; mangled.
haggler: One who cavils, hesitates and makes difficulty in bargaining.
haggling: Hacking; mangling; caviling and hesitating in bargaining.
hagiographer: A writer of holy or sacred books.
hagiography: Sacred writings. The Jews divide the books of the Scriptures into three parts; the Law, which is contained in the five first books of the Old Testament; the Prophets, or Nevim; and the Cetuvim, or writings, by way of eminence. The latter class is called by the Greeks Hagiographa, comprehending the books of Psalms, Proverbs, Job, Daniel, Ezra, Nehemiah, Ruth, Esther, Chronicles, Canticles, Lamentations, and Ecclesiastes .
hagish: Of the nature of a hag; deformed; ugly; horrid.
hagship: The state or title of a hag or witch.
hah: an exclamation expressing surprise or effort.
hail: To call; to call to a person at a distance, to arrest his attention. It is properly used in any case where the person accosted is distant, but is appropriately used by seamen. Hoa or hoi, the ship ahoay, is the usual manner of hailing; to which the answer is holloa, or hollo. Then follow the usual questions, whence came ye? where are you bound? etc.

Masses of ice or frozen vapor, falling from the clouds in showers or storms. These masses consist of little spherules united, but not all of the same consistence; some being as hard and solid as perfect ice; others soft, like frozen snow. Hailstones assume various figures; some are round, others angular, others pyramidical, others flat, and sometimes they are stellated with six radii, like crystals of snow.
To pour down masses of ice or frozen vapors.
Sound; whole; healthy; not impaired by disease; as a hail body; hail corn.
hailed: Called to from a distance; accosted.
hailing: Saluting; calling to from a distance.
hailshot: Small shot which scatter like hailstones.
hailstone: A single mass of ice falling from a cloud.
haily: Consisting of hail; as haily showers.
hainous: Properly, hateful; odious. Hence, great, enormous, aggravated; as a hainous sin or crime.
hainousness: Odiousness; enormity; as the hainousness of theft or robbery of any crime.
hair: A small filament issuing from the skin of an animal, and from a bulbous root. Each filament contains a tube or hollow within, occupied by a pulp or pith, which is intended for its nutrition, and extends only to that part which is in a state of growth. When hair means a single filament, it has a plural.

The collection or mass of filaments growing from the skin of an animal, and forming an integument or covering; as the hair of the head. Hair is the common covering of many beasts. When the filaments are very fine and short, the collection of them is called fur. Wool, also, is a kind of hair. When hair signifies a collection of these animal filaments, it has no plural.
Any thing very small or fine; or a very small distance; the breadth of a hair. He judges to a hair, that is, very exactly.
A trifling value. It is not worth a hair.
Course; order; grain; the hair falling in a certain direction. You go against the hair of your profession.
hairbell: A plant, a species of hyacinth.
hair-breadth: The diameter or breadth of a hair; a very small distance. --Seven hundred chosen men left-handed; every one could sling stones to a hair-breadth. Judges 20:16. It is used as an adjective; as a hair-breadth escape. But in New England, it is generally hair’s breadth.
haircloth: Stuff or cloth made of hair, or in part with hair. In military affairs, pieces of this cloth are used for covering the powder in wagons, or on batteries, or for covering charged bombs
hairlace: A fillet for tying up the hair of the head.
hairless: Destitute of hair; bald; as hairless scalps.
hairiness: The state of abounding or being covered with hair.
hairpin: A pin used in dressing the hair.
hairpowder: A fine powder of flour for sprinkling the hair of the head.
hair-salt: A mixture of the sulphates of magnesia and iron; its taste resembles that of alum.
hairworm: A genus of worms called Gordius; a filiform animal found in fresh water or in the earth. There are several species.
hairy: Overgrown with hair; covered with hair; abounding with hair. Esau, my brother, is a hairy man. Genesis 27:11.
Consisting of hair; as hairy honors.
Resembling hair; of the nature of hair.
hake: A kind of fish, the Gadus merlucius; called by some authors lucius marinus. It was formerly salted and dried.
halberd: A military weapon, consisting of a pole or shaft of wood, with a head armed with a steel point, with a cross piece of steel, flat and pointed at both ends, or with a cutting edge at one end, and a bent point at the other. It is carried by sergeants of foot and dragoons.
halberdier: One who is armed with a halberd.
halcyon: hal’shon. The name anciently given to the king-fisher, otherwise called alcedo; a bird that was said to lay her eggs in nests, on rocks near the sea, during the calm weather about the winter solstice. Hence,

Calm; quiet; peaceful; undisturbed; happy. Halcyon days were seven days before and as many after the winter solstice, when the weather was calm. Hence by halcyon days are now understood days of peace and tranquility.
hale: To pull or draw with force; to drag. This is now more generally written and pronounced haul, which see. It is always to be pronounced haul.
Sound; entire; healthy; robust; not impaired; as a hale body.
half: plu. halvesh'avz. One equal part of a thing which is divided into two parts, either in fact or in contemplation; a moiety; as half a pound; half a tract of land; half an orange; half the miseries or pleasures of life. It is applied to quantity, number, length, and every thing susceptible of division. In practice, of is often or usually omitted after half. We say, half a pound; half a mile; half the number. Half the misery of life.
In part, or in an equal part of degree. Half loth, and half consenting. In composition, half denotes an equal part; or indefinitely, a part, and hence.
halfblood: Relation between persons born of the same father or of the same mother, but not of both; as a brother or sister of the halfblood. The word is sometimes used as an adjective.
half-blooded: Proceeding from a male and female, each of full blood, but of different breeds; as a half-blooded sheep.
half-cap: A cap not wholly put on.
half-faced: Showing only part of the face.
half-heard: Imperfectly heard; not heard to the end. And leave half-heard the melancholy tale.
half-mark: A coin; a noble, or 6s. 8d. sterling.
half-moon: The moon at the quarters, when half its disk appears illuminated.
Any thing in the shape of a half-moon. In fortification, an outwork composed of two faces, forming a salient angle, whose gorge is in the form of a crescent or half-moon.
half-pay: Half the amount of wages or salary; as, an officer retires on half-pay.
Receiving or entitled to half-pay; as half-pay officer.
half-penny: hap’penny or ha’penny. A copper coin of the value of half a penny; also, the value of half a penny. It is used in the plural. He cheats for half-pence.
half-pike: A small pike carried by officers.
Asmall pike used in boarding ships.
half-pint: The half of a pint, or fourth of a quart.
half-scholar: One imperfectly learned. Half-seas over, a low expression denoting half drunk.
half-sword: Within half the length of a sword; close fight.
half-way: In the middle; at half the distance.
Equally distant from the extremes; as a half-way house.
half-wit: A foolish person; a dolt; a blockhead.
halibut: A fish of the genus Pleuronectes, and order of Thoracics. This fish has a compressed body, one side resembling the back, the other the belly; and both eyes on the same side of the head. It grows to a great size; some to the weight of 300 or pounds. It forms an article of food, and some parts of the body are fat, tender and delicious. This fish swims on its side, and hence the name of the genus.
hall: In architecture, a large room at the entrance of a house or palace. In the houses of ministers of state, magistrates, it is the place where they give audience and dispatch business.

An edifice in which courts of justice are held; as Westminster Hall, which was originally a royal palace, the kings of England formerly holding their parliaments and courts of judicature in their own dwellings, as is still the practice in Spain.
A manor-house, in which courts were formerly held.
A college, or large edifice belonging to a collegiate institution.
A room for a corporation or public assembly; as a town-hall; Fanueil Hall in Boston.
halleluiah: Praise ye Jehovah; give praise to God; a word used in songs or praise, or a term of rejoicing in solemn ascriptions of thanksgiving to God. It is used as a noun, or as an exclamation.
halliard: A rope or tackle for hoisting or lowering a sail.
hallier: A particular kind of net for catching birds.
halloo: To cry out; to exclaim with a loud voice; to call to by name, or by the word halloo. Country folks hallooed and hooted after me.
To encourage with shouts. Old John hallooes his hounds again.
an exclamation, used as a call to invite attention.
hallooing: Crying out; as a noun, a loud outcry.
hallow: To make holy; to consecrate; to set apart for holy or religious use. Exodus 28:38; Exodus 29:1; 1 Kings 8:64.
To devote to holy or religious exercises; to treat as sacred. Hallow the sabbath day, to do no work therein. Jeremiah 17:24.
To reverence; to honor as sacred. Hallowed be thy name.
hallowed: Consecrated to a sacred use, or to religious exercises; treated as sacred; reverenced.
hallowing: Setting apart for sacred purposes; consecrating; devoting to religious exercises; reverencing.
hallucination: In medicine, faulty sense or erroneous imagination. Hallucinations of the senses, arise from some defect in the organs of sense, or from some unusual circumstances attending the object, as when it is seen by moonlight; and they are sometimes symptoms of general disease, as in fevers. Maniacal hallucinations arise from some imaginary or mistaken idea. Similar hallucinations occur in revery.
halo: A circle appearing round the body of the sun, moon or stars, called also Corona, or crown. Halos are sometimes white and sometimes colored. Sometimes one only appears, and sometimes several concentric circles appear at the same time.
halse: hals. To embrace about the neck; to adjure; to greet.
halsening: Sounding harshly in the throat or tongue.
halser: hawz’er. A large rope of a size between the cable and the tow-line.
halt: To stop in walking; to hold. In military affairs, the true sense is retained, to stop in a march. The army halted at noon.
To limp; that is, to stop with lameness.
To hesitate; to stand in doubt whether to proceed, or what to do. How long halt ye between two opinions? 7 Kings 18:21.
To fail; to falter; as a halting sonnet.
To stop; to cause to cease marching; a military term. The general halted his troops for refreshment.
halter: A rope or strap and head-stall for leading or confining a horse.
To put a halter on; as, to halter a horse.
halve: h’av. To divide into two equal parts; as, to halve an apple.
halved: In botany, hemispherical; covering one side; placed on one side.
halves: of half. Two equal parts of a thing. To cry halves, is to claim an equal share. To go halves, is to have an equal share.
ham: Sax. ham, a house, is our modern word home, G. heim. It is used in hamlet, and in the names of places, as in Walt-ham, wood-house, walt, a wood, and ham, a house, Bucking-ham, Notting-ham, Wrent-ham, Dur-ham
The inner or hind part of the knee; the inner angle of the joint which unites the thigh and the leg of an animal. Hence,
The thigh of a beast, particularly of a hog, whether salted and cured or not. But the word is more generally understood to mean the thigh of a hog salted and dried in smoke.
hamadryad: A wood nymph, feigned to live and die with the tree to which it was attached.
hame: hames. A kind or collar for a draught horse, consisting of two bending pieces of wood or bows, and these placed on curving pads or stuffed leather, made to conform to the shape of the neck.
hamite: The fossil remains of a curved shell.
hamlet: A small village; a little cluster of houses in the country. This word seems originally to have signified the seat of a freeholder, comprehending the mansion house and adjacent buildings. It now denotes a small collection of houses in the country, in distinction from a city, a large town or township. The country wasted and the hamlets burned.
hamleted: Accustomed to a hamlet, or to a country life.
hammer: An instrument for driving nails, beating metals, and the like. It consists of an iron head, fixed crosswise to a handle. Hammers are of various sizes; a large hammer used by smiths is called a sledge.
To beat with a hammer; as, to hammer iron or steel.
To form or forge with a hammer; to shape by beating.
To work in the mind; to contrive by intellectual labor; usually with out; as, to hammer out a scheme.' .
To work; to be busy; to labor in contrivance.
hammerable: That may be shaped by a hammer.
hammercloth: The cloth which covers a coach-box, so called from the old practice of carrying a hammer, nailsin a little pocket hid by this cloth.
hammerer: One who works with a hammer.
hammerhard: Iron or steel hardened by hammering.
hammering: Beating with a hammer; working; contriving.
hammer-man: One who beats or works with a hammer.
hammoc: A kind of hanging bed, suspended between trees or posts, or by hooks. It consists of a piece of hempen cloth about six feet long and three feet wide, gathered at the ends and suspended by cords. It forms a bed, or a receptacle for a bed, on board of ships.
hamous: Hooked; having the end hooked or curved; a term of botany.
hamper: A large basket for conveying things to market.
Fetters, or some instrument that shackles.
To shackle; to entangle; hence, to impede in motion or progress, or to render progress difficult. A lion hampered in a net.
To ensnare; to inveigle; to catch with allurements.
hamster: A species of rat, the Mus cricetus, or German marmot. This rat is of the size of the water rat, but is of a browner color, and its belly and legs of a dirty yellow. It is remarkable for two bags, like those of a baboon, on each side of the jaw, under the skin, in which it conveys grain, peas and acorns to its winter residence.
hamstring: pret. and pp. hamstrung or hamstringed. To cut the tendons of the ham, and this to lame or disable.
hanaper: The hanaper was used in early days by the kings of England, for holding and carrying with them their money, as they journeyed from place to place. It was a kind of basket, like the fiscus, and hence came to be considered as the king’s treasury. Hence, the clerk or warden of the hanaper, is an officer who receives the fees due to the king for seals of charters, patents, commissions, and writs. There is also an officer who is controller of the hanaper. This word therefore answered to the modern exchequer.
hances: In architecture, the ends of elliptical arches, which are the arches of smaller circles than the scheme or middle part of the arch.
In a ship, falls of the fife-rails placed on balusters on the poop and quarter-deck down to the gangway.
hand: In man, the extremity of the arm, consisting of the palm and fingers, connected with the arm at the wrist; the part with which we hold and use any instrument.
In falconry, the foot of a hawk; and in the manege, the fore-foot of a horse.
A measure of four inches; a palm applied chiefly to horses; as a horse 14 hands high.
Side; part; right or left; as on the one hand or the other. This is admitted on all hands, that is, on all sides, or by all parties.
Act; deed; performance; external action; that is, the effect for the cause, the hand being the instrument of action. Thou sawest the contradiction between my heart and hand.
handball: An ancient game with a ball.
handbarrow: A barrow or vehicle borne by the hands of men, and without a wheel.
handbell: A small bell rung by the hand; a table bell.
handbreadth: A space equal to the breadth of the hand; a palm. Exodus 25:25.
handcuff: A manacle, consisting of iron rings for the wrists, and a connecting chain to confine the hands.
To manacle; to confine the hands with handcuffs.
handcraft: Work performed by the hands; usually written handicraft.
handed: Given or transmitted by the hands; conducted; furled.

In composition, as right-handed, most dextrous or strong with the right hand; having the right hand most able and ready. Left-handed, having the left hand most strong and convenient for principal use.
hander: One who hands or transmits; a conveyer in succession.
handfast: Hold; custody; power of confining or keeping.
handfasting: A kind of betrothing, or marriage contract.
hand-fetter: A fetter for the hand; a manacle.
handful: As much as the hand will grasp or contain.
As much as the arms will embrace.
A small quantity or number. A handful of men.
As much as can be done; full employment. In America, the phrase is, he has his hands full.€
handgallop: A slow and easy gallop, in which the hand presses the bridle to hinder increase of speed.
handglass: In gardening, a glass used for placing over, protecting and forwarding various plants, in winter.
hand-grenade: A grenade to be thrown by the hand.
handgun: A gun to be used by the hand.
handicraft: Manual occupation; work performed by the hand.
A man who obtains his living by manual labor; one skilled in some mechanical art.
handicraftsman: A man skilled or employed in manual occupation; a manufacturer.
handily: With dexterity or skill; dexterously; adroitly.
handiness: The ease of performance derived from practice; dexterity; adroitness.
handiwork: Work of the hands; product of manual labor; manufacture.
Work performed by power and wisdom. Psalm 19:1.
handkerchief: A piece of cloth, usually silk or linen, carried about the person for the purpose of cleaning the face or hands, as occasion requires.
A piece of cloth to be worn about the neck, and sometimes called a neckerchief.
handlanguage: The art of conversing by the hands.
handle: To touch; to feel with the hand; to use or hold with the hand. The bodies we daily handle--hinder the approach of the part of our hands that press them.
To manage; to use; to wield. That fellow handles a bow like a crow-keeper.
To make familiar by frequent touching. The breeders in Flanders--handle their colts six months every year.
To treat; to discourse on; to discuss; to use or manage in writing or speaking. The author handled the subject with address. The speaker handled the arguments to the best advantage.
To use; to deal with; to practice. They that handle the law knew me not. Jeremiah 2:8.
handmaid: A maid that waits at hand; a female servant or attendant.
handmaiden: A maid that waits at hand; a female servant or attendant.
handmill: A mill moved by the hand.
handsaw: A saw to be used with the hand.
handscrew: An engine for raising heavy timbers or weights; a jack.
handsel: The first act of using any thing; the first sale.
An earnest; money for the first sale.
To use or do any thing the first time.
handsome: Properly, dexterous; ready; convenient. For a thief it is so handsome, as it may seem it was first invented for him. This sense is either from the original meaning of hand, or from the use of the hand, or rather of the right hand. In this sense the word is still used. We say of a well fought combat and victory, it is a handsome affair, an affair well performed, done with dexterity or skill.

Moderately beautiful, as the person or other thing; well made; having symmetry of parts; well formed. It expresses less than beautiful or elegant; as a handsome woman or man; she has a handsome person or face. So we say, a handsome house; a handsome type.
Graceful in manner; marked with propriety and ease; as a handsome address.
Neat; correct; moderately elegant; as a handsome style or composition.
handsomely: Neatly; with due symmetry or proportions; as, a thing is handsomely made or finished.
With a degree of beauty; as a room handsomely furnished or ornamented.
Amply; generously; liberally. She is handsomely endowed.
handsomeness: A moderate degree of beauty or elegance; as the handsomeness of the person or of an edifice.
Grace; gracefulness; ease and propriety in manner.
handspike: A wooden bar, used with the hand as a lever, for various purposes, as in raising weights, heaving about a windlass
handvise: A vise used by hand, or for small work.
handweapon: Any weapon to be wielded by the hand. Numbers 35:18.
handwriting: The cast or form of writing peculiar to each hand or person.
handy: Performed by the hand. They came to handy blows.
Dexterous; ready; adroit; skilled to use the hands with ease in performance; applied to persons. He is handy with the saw or the place. Each is handy in his way.
Ingenious; performing with skill and readiness.
Ready to the hand; near. My books are very handy.
Convenient; suited to the use of the hand.
handyblow: A blow with the hand; an act of hostility.
handy-dandy: A play in which children change hands and places.
handystroke: A blow inflicted by the hand.
hang: pret. and pp. hanged or hung.
To suspend; to fasten to some fixed object above, in such a manner as to swing or move; as, to hang a thief. Pharaoh hanged the chief baker. Hence,
To put to death by suspending by the neck. Many men would rebel, rather than be ruined; but they would rather not rebel than be hanged.
To place without any solid support or foundation. He hangeth the earth upon nothing. Job 26:7.
To fix in such a manner as to be movable; as, to hang a door or grate on hooks or by butts.
hanged: Suspended; put to death by being suspended by the neck.
hanger: That by which a thing is suspended.
A short broad sword, incurvated towards the point.
One that hangs, or causes to be hanged.
hanger-on: One who besets another importunately in soliciting favors.
A dependant; one who eats and drinks without payment.
hanging: Foreboding death by the halter. What a hanging face!
Requiring punishment by the halter; as a hanging matter.
Any kind of drapery hung or fastened to the walls or a room, by way of ornament. No purple hangings clothe the palace walls.
hanging-sleeves: Strips of the same stuff with the gown, hanging down the back from the shoulders.
hanging-side: In mining, the overhanging side of an inclined or hading vein.
hangman: One who hangs another; a public executioner; also, a term of reproach.
hangnest: The name of certain species of birds, which build nests suspended from the branches of trees, such as the Baltimore oriole or red-bird; also, the nest so suspended.
hank: A skein of thread; as much thread as is tied together; a tie.
In ships. a wooden ring fixed to a stay, to confine the stay-sails; used in the place of a grommet.
A rope or withy for fastening a gate.
hanker: To long for with a keen appetite and uneasiness; in a literal sense; as, to hanker for fruit, or after fruit.
To have a vehement desire of something, accompanied with uneasiness; as, to hanker after the diversions of the town. It is usually followed by after. It is a familiar, but not a low word.
hankering: A keen appetite that causes uneasiness till it is gratified; venement desire to possess or enjoy.
Longing for with keen appetite or ardent desire.
hant: a contraction of have not, or has not; as, I ha’nt, he ha’nt, we ha’nt. Hanse Towns. Hanse signifies a society; Goth. hansa, a multitude. The Hanse towns in Germany were certain commercial cities which associated for the protection of commerce as early as the twelfth century. To this confederacy acceded certain commercial cities in Holland, England, France, Spain and Italy, until they amounted to seventy two, and for centuries, this confederacy commanded the respect and defied the power of kings. This confederacy at present consists of the cities of Lubeck, Hamburg, and Bremen .
hanseatic: Pertaining to the Hanse towns, or to their confederacy.
hap: That which comes suddenly or unexpectedly; chance; fortune; accident; casual event. Whether art it was or heedless hap. Curs’d by good haps, and curs’d be they that build . Their hopes on haps.
To happen; to befall; to come by chance.
hap-hazard: Chance; accident. We take our principles at hap-hazard on trust.
hapless: Luckless; unfortunate; unlucky; unhappy; as hapless youth; hapless maid.
haply: By chance; perhaps; it may be. Lest haply ye be found to fight against God. Acts 5:39.
happen: To come by chance; to come without one’s previous expectation; to fall out. There shall no evil happen to the just. Proverbs 12:21.
To come; to befall. They talked together of all those things which had happened. Luke 24:14.
To light; to fall or come unexpectedly. I have happened on some other accounts relating to mortalities.
happily: By good fortune; fortunately; luckily; with success. Preferr'd by conquest, happily o’erthrown.
In a happy state; in a state of felicity. He lived happily with his consort.
With address or dexterity; gracefully; in a manner to ensure success. Formed by thy converse, happily to steer WWV”AD . From grave to gay, from lively to severe.
happiness: The agreeable sensations which spring from the enjoyment of good; that state of a being in which his desires are gratified, by the enjoyment of pleasure without pain; felicity; but happiness usually expresses less than felicity, and felicity less than bliss. Happiness is comparative. To a person distressed with pain, relief from that pain affords happiness; in other cases we give the name happiness to positive pleasure or an excitement of agreeable sensations. Happiness therefore admits of indefinite degrees of increase in enjoyment, or gratification of desires. Perfect happiness, or pleasure unalloyed with pain, is not attainable in this life.
Fortuitous elegance; unstudied grace. For there’s a happiness as well as care.
happy: Lucky; fortunate; successful. Chimists have been more happy in finding experiments, than the causes of them. So we say, a happy thought; a happy expedient.

Being in the enjoyment of agreeable sensations from the possession of good; enjoying pleasure from the gratification of appetites or desires. The pleasurable sensations derived from the gratification of sensual appetites render a person temporarily happy; but he only can be esteemed really and permanently happy, who enjoys peace of mind in the favor of God. To be in any degree happy, we must be free from pain both of body and of mind; to be very happy, we must be in the enjoyment of lively sensations of pleasure, either of body or mind. Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed. Genesis 30:13. He found himself happiest, in communicating happiness to others.
Prosperous; having secure possession of good. Happy is that people whose God is Jehovah. Psalm 144:15.
That supplies pleasure; that furnishes enjoyment; agreeable; applied to things; as a happy condition.
Dexterous; ready; able. One gentleman is happy at a reply, another excels in a rejoinder.
harangue: A speech addressed to an assembly or an army; a popular oration; a public address. This word seems to imply loudness or declamation, and is therefore appropriated generally to an address made to a popular assembly or to an army, and not to a sermon, or to an argument at the bar of a court, or to a speech in a deliberative council, unless in contempt.
Declamation; a noisy, pompous or irregular address.
harang’. To make an address or speech to a large assembly; to make a noisy speech.
harang’. To address by oration; as, the general harangued the troops.
haranguer: harang’er. An orator; one who addresses an assembly or army; a noisy declaimer.
harass: To weary; to fatigue to excess; to tire with bodily labor; as, to harass an army by a long march.
To weary with importunity, care, or perplexity; to tease; to perplex. Nature oppress’d and harrass’d out with care.
harasser: One who harasses or teases; a spoiler.
harbinger: In England, an officer of the king’s household who rides a day’s journey before the court when traveling, to provide lodgings and other accommodations.
A forerunner; a precursor; that which precedes and gives notice of the expected arrival of something else.
harbor: A lodging; a place of entertainment and rest. For harbor at a thousand doors they knocked.
A port or haven for ships; a bay or inlet of the sea, in which ships can moor, and be sheltered from the fury of winds and a heavy sea; any navigable water where ships can ride in safety.
An asylum; a shelter; a place of safety from storms or danger.
To shelter; to secure; to secrete; as, to harbor a thief.
To entertain; to permit to lodge, rest or reside; as, to harbor malice or revenge. Harbor not a thought of revenge.
harborer: One who entertains or shelters another.
harborless: Without a harbor; destitute of shelter or a lodging.
harbor-master: An officer who has charge of the mooring of ships, and executes the regulations respecting harbors.
hard: Firm; solid; compact; not easily penetrated, or separated into parts; not yielding to pressure; applied to material bodies, and opposed to soft; as hard wood; hard flesh; a hard apple.
Difficult; not easy to the intellect. In which are some things hard to be understood. 2 Peter 3:16. The hard causes they brought to Moses. Exodus 18:26.
Difficult of accomplishment; not easy to be done or executed. A hard task; a disease hard to cure. Is any thing too hard for the Lord? Genesis 18:14.
Full of difficulties or obstacles; not easy to be traveled; as a hard way.
Painful; difficult; distressing. Rachel travailed, and she had hard labor. Genesis 35:16.
hardbound: Costive; fast or tight; as hardbound brains.
harden: h‘ardn. To make hard or more hard; to make firm or compact; to indurate; as, to harden iron or steel; to harden clay.
h’ardn. To become hard or more hard; to acquire solidity or more compactness. Mortar hardens by drying.
To confirm in effrontery; to make impudent; as, to harden the face.
To make obstinate, unyielding or refractory; as, to harden the neck. Jeremiah 19:15.

To confirm in wickedness, opposition or enmity; to make obdurate. Why then do ye harden your hearts, as Pharaoh and the Egyptians hardened their hearts? 7 Samuel 6:6. So God is said to harden the heart, when he withdraws the influences of his spirit from men, and leaves them to pursue their own corrupt inclinations.
hardened: Made hard, or more hard or compact; made unfeeling; made obstinate; confirmed in error or vice.
hardener: He or that which makes hard, or more firm and compact.
hardening: Making hard or more compact; making obdurate or unfeeling; confirming; becoming more hard.
The giving a greater degree of hardness to bodies than they had before.
hardfavored: Having coarse features; harsh of countenance.
hardfought: Vigorously contested; as a hard-fought battle.
hardhanded: Having hard hands, as a laborer.
hardhead: Clash or collision of heads in contest.
hardhearted: Cruel; pitiless; merciless; unfeeling; inhuman; inexorable.
hardheartedness: Want of feeling or tenderness; cruelty; inhumanity.
hardihood: Boldness, united with firmness and constancy of mind; dauntless bravery; intrepidity. It is the society of numbers which gives hardihood to iniquity. Hardihead and hardiment, in the sense of hardihood, are obsolete.
hardiness: Boldness; firm courage; intrepidity; stoutness; bravery; applied to the mind, it is synonymous with hardihood.
Firmness of body derived from laborious exercises.
hard-labored: Wrought with severe labor; elaborate; studies; as a hard-labored poem.
hardly: With difficulty; with great labor. Recovering hardly what he lost before.
Scarcely; barely; almost not. Hardly shall you find any one so bad, but he desires the credit of being thought good.
Not quite or wholly. The object is so distant we can hardly see it. The veal is hardly done. The writing is hardly completed.
Severely; unfavorably; as, to think hardly of public measures.
hard-mouthed: Not sensible to the bit; not easily governed; as a hard-mouthed horse.
hardness: Firmness; close union of the component parts; compactness; solidity; the quality of bodies which resists impression; opposed to softness and fluidity.
Difficulty to be executed or accomplished; as the hardness of an enterprise.
Scarcity; penury; difficulty of obtaining money; as the hardness of the times.
Obduracy; impenitence; confirmed state of wickedness; as hardness of heart.
hardnibbed: Having a hard nib or point.
hardock: Probably hoardock, dock with whitish leaves.
hards: The refuse or coarse part of flax; tow.
hardship: Toil; fatigue; severe labor or want; whatever oppresses the body.
hardvisaged: Having coarse features; of a harsh countenance.
hardware: Wares made of iron or other metal, as pots, kettles, saws, knives
hardwareman: A maker or seller of hardwares.
hardy: Bold; brave; stout; daring; resolute; intrepid. Who is hardy enough to encounter contempt?
Strong; firm; compact. An unwholesome blast may shake in pieces his hardy fabric.
Confident; full of assurance; impudent; stubborn to excess.
Inured to fatigue; rendered firm by exercise, as a veteran soldier.
har: in composition, signify an army, Sax. here, G. heer, D. heir. So Harold is a general of an army; Herwin, a victorious army.
hare: in composition, signify an army, Sax. here, G. heer, D. heir. So Harold is a general of an army; Herwin, a victorious army.

A quadruped of the genus Lepus, with long ears, a short tail, soft hair, and a divided upper lip. It is a timid animal, often hunted for sport or for its flesh, which is excellent food. It moves by leaps, and is remarkable for its fecundity.
To fright, or to excite, tease and harass, or worry.
here: in composition, signify an army, Sax. here, G. heer, D. heir. So Harold is a general of an army; Herwin, a victorious army.
In this place; in the place where the speaker is present; opposed to there. Behold, here am I. Lodge here this night. Build here seven altars.
In the present life or state. Thus shall you be happy here, and more happy hereafter.
It is used in making an offer or attempt. Then here’s for earnest.

In drinking health. Here’s to thee, Dick. It is neither here nor there, it is neither in this place nor in that; neither in one place nor in another. Here and there, in one place and another; in a dispersed manner or condition; thinly; or irregularly.
harebell: A plant of the genus Hyacinthus, with campaniform or bell-shaped flowers.
harehunter: One who hunts or is used to hunting hares.
harelip: A divided upper lip, like that of a hare.
harem: A seraglio; a place where Eastern princes confine their women, who are prohibited from the society of others.
haricot: A kind of ragout of meat and roots.
harier: A dog for hunting hares; a kind of hound with an acute sense of smelling.
harrier: A dog for hunting hares; a kind of hound with an acute sense of smelling.
A hunting hound with a nice sense of smelling.
hark: To listen; to lend the ear. This word is rarely or never used, except in the imperative mode, hark, that is, listen.
harl: The skin of flax; the filaments of flax or hemp.
herl: The skin of flax; the filaments of flax or hemp.
harlequin: A buffoon, dressed in party-colored clothes, who plays tricks, like a merry-andrew, to divert the populace. This character was first introduced into Italian comedy, but is now a standing character in English pantomime entertainments.
To play the droll; to make sport by playing ludicrous tricks.
harlot: A woman who prostitutes her body for hire; a prostitute; a common woman.
In Scripture, one who forsakes the true God and worships idols. Isaiah 1:21.
A servant; a rogue; a cheat.
harlotry: The trade or practice of prostitution; habitual or customary lewdness.
harm: Injury; hurt; damage; detriment. Do thyself no harm. Acts 16:28. He shall make amends for the harm he hath done in the holy thing. Leviticus 5:16.
Moral wrong; evil; mischief; wickedness; a popular sense of the word.
To hurt; to injure; to damage; to impair soundness of body, either animal or vegetable.
harmattan: A dry easterly wind in Africa, which destroys vegetation.
harmful: Hurtful; injurious; noxious; detrimental; mischievous. The earth brought forth fruit and food for man, without any mixture of harmful quality.
harmless: Not hurtful or injurious; innoxious. Ceremonies are harmless in themselves.
Unhurt; undamaged; uninjured; as, to give bond to save another harmless.
Innocent; not guilty. Who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners. Hebrews 7:26.
harmlessly: Innocently; without fault or crime; as, to pass the time harmlessly in recreations.
Without hurt or damage. Bullets fall harmlessly into wood or feathers.
harmlessness: The quality of being innoxious; freedom from a tendency to injure.
harmonic: Relating to harmony or music; as harmonical use.
Concordant; musical; consonant; as harmonic sounds. Harmonic twang of leather, horn and brass. The basis of an harmonic system. The harmonic elements are the three smallest concords.

An epithet applied to the accessary sounds which accompany the predominant and apparently simple tone of any chord or string. Harmonical mean, in arithmetic and algebra, a term used to express certain relations of numbers and quantities, which are supposed to bear an analogy to musical consonances. Harmonical proportion, in arithmetic and algebra, is said to obtain between three quantities, or four quantities, in certain cases. Harmonical series, a series of many numbers in continued harmonical proportion.
harmonical: Relating to harmony or music; as harmonical use.
Concordant; musical; consonant; as harmonic sounds. Harmonic twang of leather, horn and brass. The basis of an harmonic system. The harmonic elements are the three smallest concords.

An epithet applied to the accessary sounds which accompany the predominant and apparently simple tone of any chord or string. Harmonical mean, in arithmetic and algebra, a term used to express certain relations of numbers and quantities, which are supposed to bear an analogy to musical consonances. Harmonical proportion, in arithmetic and algebra, is said to obtain between three quantities, or four quantities, in certain cases. Harmonical series, a series of many numbers in continued harmonical proportion.
harmonica: A collection of musical glasses of a particular form, so arranged as to produce exquisite music.
harmonics: The doctrine or science of musical sounds.

Derivative sounds, generated with predominant sounds, and produced by subordinate vibrations of a chord or string, when its whole length vibrates. These shorter vibrations produce more acute sounds, and are called acute harmonics.
Grave harmonics are low sounds which accompany every perfect consonance of two sounds.
harmonious: Adapted to each other; having the parts proportioned to each other; symmetrical. God hath made the intellectual world harmonious and beautiful without us.
Concordant; consonant; symphonious; musical. Harmonious sounds are such as accord, and are agreeable to the ear.
Agreeing; living in peace and friendship; as a harmonious family or society .
harmoniously: With just adaptation and proportion of parts to each other. Distances, motions, and quantities of matter harmoniously adjusted in this great variety of our system.
With accordance of sound; musically; in concord.
In agreement; in peace and friendship.
harmoniousness: Proportion and adaption of parts; musicalness.
harmonist: A musician; a composer of music.
One who brings together corresponding passages, to show their agreement.
harmonize: To be in concord; to agree in sounds.
To agree; to be in peace and friendship; as individuals or families.
To agree in sense or purport; as, the arguments harmonize; the facts stated by different witnesses harmonize.
To adjust in fit proportions; to cause to agree.
To make musical; to combine according to the laws of counterpoint.
harmonizer: One that brings together or reconciles.
harmonometer: An instrument or monochord for measuring the harmonic relations var of sounds.
harmony: The just adaptation of parts to each other, in any system or composition of things, intended to form a connected whole; as the harmony of the universe. Equality and correspondence are the causes of harmony. All discord, harmony not understood.

Just proportion of sound; consonance; musical concord; the accordance of two or more intervals or sounds, or that union of different sounds which pleases the ear; or a succession of such sounds, called chords. Ten thousand harps that tuned Angelic harmonies.
Concord; agreement; accordance in facts; as the harmony of the gospels.
Concord or agreement in views, sentiments or manners, interests, good correspondence; peace and friendship. The citizens live in harmony.

Natural harmony, in music, consists of the harmonic triad or common chord. Artificial harmony, is a mixture of concords and discords. Figured harmony, is when one or more of the parts move, during the continuance of a chord, through certain notes which do not form any of the constituent parts of that chord.
harmost: In ancient Greece, a Spartan governor, regulator or perfect.
harmotome: In mineralogy, cross-stone, or staurolite, called also pyramidical zeolite.
harness: Armor; the whole accouterments or equipments of a knight or horseman; originally perhaps defensive armor, but in a more modern and enlarged sense, the furniture of a military man, or offensive, aS a casque, cuirass, helmet, girdle, sword, buckler.
The furniture of a draught horse, whether for a wagon, coach, gig, chaise, called in some of the American states, tackle or tackling, with which, in its primary sense, it is synonymous.
To dress in armor; to equip with armor for war, as a horseman. Harnessed in rugged steel.
To put on the furniture of a horse for draught. Harness the horses. Jeremiah 46:4.
To defend; to equip or furnish for defense. 1 Maccabees 4:7.
harnessed: Equipped with armor; furnished with the dress for draught; defended.
harnesser: One who puts on the harness of a horse.
harnessing: Putting on armor or furniture for draught.
harp: An instrument of music of the stringed kind, of a triangular figure, held upright and commonly touched with the fingers.
To play on the harp. I heard the voice of harpers, harping with their harps. Revelation 14:2.
To dwell on, in speaking or writing; to continue sounding. He seems . Proud and disdainful, harping on what I am-- . Not what he knew I was.
To touch as a passion; to affect.
harping: Playing on a harp; dwelling on continually.
A continual dwelling on. Making infinite merriment by harpings upon old themes.

harpings. In ships, harpings are the fore-parts of the wales, which encompass the bow of the ship, and are fastened to the stem. Their use is to strengthen the ship, in the place where she sustains the greatest shock in plunging into the sea. Cat-harpings, are ropes which serve to brace in the shrouds of the lower masts, behind their respective yards.
harpoon: A harping-iron; a spear or javelin, used to strike whales for killing them. It consists of a long shank, with a broad flat triangular head, sharpened at both edges for penetrating the whale with facility. It is generally thrown by hand.
To strike, catch or kill with a harpoon. The beluga is usually caught in nets, but is sometimes harpooned.
harpooned: Struck, caught or killed with a harpoon.
harpooner: One who uses a harpoon; the man in a whale-boat who throws the harpoon.
harpsichord: An instrument of music with strings of wire, played by the fingers, by means of keys. The striking of these keys moves certain little jacks, which move a double row of chords or strings, stretched over four bridges on the table of the instrument.
harpy: In antiquity, the harpies were fabulous winged monsters, having the face of a woman and the body of a vulture, with their feet and fingers armed with sharp claws. They were three in number, Aello, Ocypete, and Celeno. They were sent by Juno to plunder the table of Phineus. They are represented as rapacious and filthy animals.
Any rapacious or ravenous animal; an extortioner; a plunderer.
harrateen: A kind of stuff or cloth.
harrow: An instrument of agriculture, formed of pieces of timber sometimes crossing each other, and set with iron teeth. It is drawn over plowed land to level it and break the clods, and to cover seed when sown.
To draw a harrow over, for the purpose of breaking clods and leveling the surface, or for covering seed sown; as, to harrow land or ground.
To break or tear with a harrow. Will he harrow the valleys after thee? Job 39:10.
To tear; to lacerate; to torment. I could a tale unfold, whose lightest word . Would harrow up thy soul--\WWV”AD .
To pillage; to strip; to lay waste by violence.
harrowed: Broken or smoothed by a harrow.
harrowing: Breaking or leveling with a harrow.
harry: To harass; to agitate; to tease.
harsh: Rough to the touch; rugged; grating; as harsh sand; harsh cloth; opposed to smooth.
Sour; rough to the taste; as harsh fruit.
Rough to the ear; grating; discordant; jarring; as a harsh sound; harsh notes; a harsh voice.
Austere; crabbed; morose; peevish. Civilization softens the harsh temper or nature of man.
Rough; rude; abusive; as harsh words; a harsh reflection.
harshly: Severely; morosely; crabbedly; as, to speak or answer harshly.
Roughly; rudely; with violence; as, to treat a person harshly.
Roughly; with a grating sound; unpleasantly. It would sound harshly in her ears.
harshness: Roughness to the touch; opposed to softness and smoothness.
Sourness; austereness; as the harshness of fruit.
Roughness to the ear; as the harshness of sound or of a voice, or of verse. ‘Tis not enough no harshness gives offense, The sound must seem an echo to the sense.
Roughness of temper; moroseness; crabbedness; peevishness.
Roughness in manner or words; severity; as the harshness of reproof.
harslet: The heart, liver, lightsof a hog.
haslet: The heart, liver, lightsof a hog.
hart: A stag or male deer, an animal of the cervine genus.
hartbeest: The quanga, or cervine antelope of Africa.
hartshorn: The horn of the hart or male deer. The scrapings or raspings of this horn are medicinal, and used in decoctions, ptisansHartshorn jelly is nutritive and strengthening. Hartshorn calcined by a strong and long continued heat, is changed into a white earth, which is employed in medicine as an absorbent. The salt of hartshorn is powerful sudorific, and hartshorn yields also a pungent volatile spirit. The jelly of hartshorn is simply gelatine; the earth remaining after calcination, is phosphate of lime; the salt and spirit of hartshorn are muriate of ammonia, with a little animal oil. Hartshorn plantain, a species of Plantago.
hartstongue: A plant, a species of Asplenium.
hartwort: The name of certain plants of the genera, Seseli, Tordylium, and Buplerum.
haruspice: In Roman history, a person who pretended to foretell future events by inspecting the entrails of beasts sacrificed, or watching the circumstances attending their slaughter, or their manner of burning and the ascent of the smoke.
haruspicy: Divination by the inspection of victims.
harvest: The season of reaping and gathering in corn or other crops. It especially refers to the time of collecting corn or grain, which is the chief food of men, as wheat and rye. In Egypt and Syria, the wheat harvest is in April and May; in the south of Europe and of the United States, in June; in the Northern states of America, in July; and in the north of Europe, in August and September. In the United States, the harvest of maiz is mostly in October.
The ripe corn or grain collected and secured in barns or stacks. The harvest this year is abundant.
The product of labor; fruit or fruits. Let us the harvest of our labor eat.
Fruit or fruits; effects; consequences. He that sows iniquity will reap a harvest of woe.

In Scripture, harvest signifies figuratively the proper season for business. He that sleepeth in harvest, is a son that causeth shame. Proverbs 10:5. Also, a people whose sins have ripened them for judgment. Joel 3:13. Also, the end of the world. Matthew 13:39. Also, a seasonable time for instructing men in the gospel. Matthew 9:37-38.
harvested: Reaped and collected, as ripe corn and fruits.
harvester: A reaper; a laborer in gathering grain.
harvest-ely: A large four-winged insect of the cicada kind, common in Italy.
harvest-home: The song sung by reapers at the feast made at the gathering of corn, or the feast itself.
harvesting: Reaping and collecting, as ripe corn and other fruits.
harvest-queen: An image representing Ceres, formerly carried about on the last day of harvest.
hash: To chop into small pieces; to mince and mix; as, to hash meat.
Minced meat, or a dish of meat and vegetables chopped into small pieces and mixed.
hask: A case made of rushes or flags.
hasp: A clasp that passes over a staple to be fastened by a padlock.
A spindle to wind thread or silk on.
hassoc: A thick mat or bass on which persons kneel in church. And knees and hassocs are well nigh divore’d.
hast: the second person singular of have, I have, thou hast, contracted from havest. It is used only in the solemn style.
hastate: In botany, spear-shaped; resembling the head of a halberd; triangular, hollowed at the base and on the sides, with the angles spreading; as a hastate leaf.
hastated: In botany, spear-shaped; resembling the head of a halberd; triangular, hollowed at the base and on the sides, with the angles spreading; as a hastate leaf.
haste: Celerity of motion; speed; swiftness; dispatch; expedition; applied only to voluntary beings, as men and other animals; never to other bodies. We never say, a ball flies with haste. The king’s business required haste.7 Samuel 21:8.
Sudden excitement of passion; quickness; precipitance; vehemence.N . I said in my haste, all men are liars. Psalm 116:11.
The state of being urged or pressed by business; as, I am in great haste.
To press; to drive or urge forward; to push on; to precipitate; to accelerate movement. I would hasten my escape from the windy storm. Psalm 55:8.
To move with celerity; to be rapid in motion; to be speedy or quick. They were troubled and hasted away. Psalm 48:5.
hasten: To press; to drive or urge forward; to push on; to precipitate; to accelerate movement. I would hasten my escape from the windy storm. Psalm 55:8.
To move with celerity; to be rapid in motion; to be speedy or quick. They were troubled and hasted away. Psalm 48:5.
hasted: Moved rapidly; accelerated; urged with speed.
hastened: Moved rapidly; accelerated; urged with speed.
hastener: One that hastens or urges forward.
hasting: Urging forward; pushing on; proceeding rapidly. That state is hastening to ruin, in which no difference is made between good and bad men.
hastening: Urging forward; pushing on; proceeding rapidly. That state is hastening to ruin, in which no difference is made between good and bad men.
hastily: In haste; with speed or quickness; speedily; nimbly. Half clothed, half naked, hastily retire.
Rashly; precipitately; without due reflection. We hastily engaged in the war.
Passionately; under sudden excitement of passion.
hastiness: Haste; speed; quickness or celerity in motion or action, as of animals.
Rashness; heedless eagerness; precipitation. Our hastiness to engage in the war caused deep regret.
Irritability; susceptibility of anger, warmth or temper.
hasting-pear: An early pear, called also green chissel.
hasty: Quick; speedy; opposed to slow. Be not hasty to go out of his sight. Ecclesiastes 8:3.
Eager precipitate; rash; opposed to deliberate. Seest thou a man that is hasty in his words? There is more hope of a fool than of him. Proverbs 29:20.
Irritable; easily excited to wrath; passionate. He that is hasty of spirit exalteth folly. Proverbs 14:29.
Early ripe; forward; as hasty fruit. Isaiah 28:4.
hastypudding: A pudding made of the meal of maiz moistened with water and boiled, or of milk and flour boiled.
hat: A covering for the head; a garment made of different materials, and worn by men or women for defending the head from rain or heat, or for ornament. Hats for men are usually made of fur or wool, and formed with a crown and brim. Hats for females are made of straw or grass braid, and various other materials. Of these the ever varying forms admit of no description that can long be correct.
hat-band: A band round the crown of a hat.
hat-box: A box for a hat. But a case for a lady’s hat is called a band- box.
hat-case: A box for a hat. But a case for a lady’s hat is called a band- box.
hatch: To produce young from eggs by incubation, or by artificial heat. In Egypt, chickens are hatched by artificial heat. The partridge sitteth on eggs and hatcheth them not. Jeremiah 17:11.
To contrive or plot; to form by meditation, and bring into being; to originate and produce in silence; as, to hatch mischief; to hatch heresy.
To shade by lines in drawing and engraving. Those hatching strokes of the pencil.
To produce young; to bring the young to maturity. Eggs will not hatch without a due degree and continuance of heat.
hatches: Properly, the grate or frame of cross-bars laid over the opening in a ship’s deck, now called hatch-bars. The lid or cover of a hatchway is also called hatches.
The opening in a ship’s deck, or the passage from one deck to another, the name of the grate itself being used for the opening; but this is more properly called the hatchway.
A half-door, or door with an opening over it.
In Cornwall, Eng. openings into mines, or in search of them.
hatchel: An instrument formed with long iron teeth set in a board, for cleaning flax or hemp from the tow, hards or coarse part. The hatchel is a large species of comb.
To draw flax or hemp through the teeth of a hatchel, for separating the coarse part and broken pieces of the stalk from the fine fibrous parts.
To tease or vex, by sarcasms or reproaches; a vulgar use of the word.
hatcheling: Drawing through the teeth of a hatchel.
hatchet: A small ax with a short handle, to be used with one hand. To take up the hatchet, a phrase borrowed from the natives of America, is to make war. To bury the hatchet, is to make peace.
hatchet-face: A prominent face, like the edge of a hatchet.
hatchetine: A substance of the hardness of soft tallow, of a yellowish white or greenish yellow color, found in South Wales.
hatchment: An armorial escutcheon on a herse at funerals, or in a church.
hatchway: In ships, a square or oblong opening in the deck, affording a passage from one deck, affording a passage from one deck to another, or into the hold or lower apartments.
hate: To dislike greatly; to have a great aversion to. It expresses less than abhor, detest, and abominate, unless pronounced with a peculiar emphasis. How long will fools hate knowledge? Proverbs 1:22. Blessed are ye when men shall hate you. Luke 6:22. The Roman tyrant was contented to be hated, if he was but feared.
In Scripture, it signifies to love less. If any man come to me, and hate not father and motherLuke 14:26. He that spareth the rod, hateth his son. Proverbs 13:24.
hateful: Odious; exciting great dislike, aversion or disgust. All sin is hateful in the sight of God and of good men.
That feels hatred; malignant; malevolent. And, worse than death, to view with hateful eyes\V\ . His rival’s conquest.
hatefulness: Odiousness; the quality of being hateful, or of exciting aversion or disgust.
hater: One that hates. An enemy to God, and hater of all good.
hating: Disliking extremely; entertaining a great aversion for.
hatred: Great dislike or aversion; hate; enmity.
