howitzer: A kind of mortar or short gun, mounted on a field carriage, and used for throwing shells. The difference between a mortar and a howitz is that the trunnions of a mortar are at the end, but those of a howitz are at the middle.
howker: A Dutch vessel with two masts, a main and a mizen-mast; also, a fishing M boat with one mast, used on the coast of Ireland.
howl: To cry as a dog or wolf; to utter a particular kind of loud, protracted and mournful sound. We say, the dog howls; the wolf howls. Hence,
To utter a loud, mournful sound, expressive of distress; to wail. Howl lye, for the day of the Lord is at hand. Isaiah 13:6. Ye rich men, weep and howl. James 5:7.
To utter or speak with outcry. Go--howl it out in deserts.
The cry of a dog or wolf, or other like sound.
howlet: A fowl of the owl kind, which utters a mournful cry. It is as large as a pullet.
howling: Uttering the cry of a dog or wolf; uttering a loud cry of distress.
Filled with howls, or howling beasts; dreary. Innumerable artifices and stratagems are acted in the howling wilderness and in the great deep, that can never come to our knowledge.
The act of howling; a loud outcry or mournful sound.
hoy: A small vessel, usually rigged as a sloop, and employed in conveying passengers and goods from place to place on the sea coast, or in transporting goods to and from a ship in a road or bay.
an exclamation, of no definite meaning.
hubbub: A great noise of many confused voices; a tumult; uproar; riot.
huck: The name of a German river-trout.
huckaback: A kind of linen with raised figures on it.
huckle: The hip, that is, a bunch.
huckster: A retailer of small articles, of provisions, nuts.
To deal in small articles, or in petty bargains.
hud: The shell or hull of a hut.
huddle: To crowd; to press together promiscuously, without order or regularity. We say of a throng of people, they huddle together.
To move in a promiscuous throng without order; to press or hurry in disorder. The people huddle along, or huddle into the house.
To put on in haste and disorder; as, she huddled on her clothes.
To cover in haste or carelessly.
To perform in haste and disorder.
huddling: Crowding or throwing together in disorder; putting on carelessly.
hue: Color; dye. Flow’rs of all hue.
in the phrase hue and cry, signifies a shouting or vociferation. In law, a hue and cry is the pursuit of a felon or offender, with loud outcries or clamor to give an alarm.
huer: One whose business is to cry out or give an alarm.
huff: A swell of sudden anger or arrogance. A Spaniard was wonderfully upon the huff about his extraction.
A boaster; one swelled with a false opinion of his own value or importance. Lewd shallow-brained huffs make atheism and contempt of religion the badge of wit.
To swell; to enlarge; to puff up.
To hector; to bully; to treat with insolence and arrogance; to chide or rebuke with insolence.
To swell; to dilate or enlarge; as, the bread huffs.
huffer: A bully; a swaggerer; a blusterer.
huffiness: Petulance; the state of being puffed up.
hug: To press close in an embrace. --And hugged me in his arms.
To embrace closely; to hold fast; to treat with fondness. We hug deformities, if they bear our names.
To gripe in wrestling or scuffling. To hug the land, in sailing, to sail as near the land as possible. To hug the wind, to keep the ship close-hauled.
A particular gripe in wrestling or scuffling.
huge: Very large or great; enormous; applied to bulk or size; as a huge mountain; a huge ox.
It is improperly applied to space and distance, in the sense of great, vast, immense; as a hugh space; a hugh difference. This is inelegant, or rather vulgar.
In colloquial language, very great; enormous; as a huge feeder.
hugely: Very greatly; enormously; immensely. Doth it not flow as hugely as the sea?
hugeness: Enormous bulk or largeness; as the hugeness of a mountain or of an elephant.
hugger-mugger: In hugger-mugger, denotes in privacy or secrecy, and the word adverbially used, denotes secretly.
humbler: He or that which humbles; he that reduces pride or mortifies.
humbly: In a humble manner; with modest submissiveness; with humility. Hope humbly the, with trembling pinions soar, Wait the great teacher, death, and God adore.
In a low state or condition; without elevation.
humboldite: A rare mineral recently described, occurring in small crystals, nearly colorless and transparent, or of a yellowish tinge and translucent; rarely separate, but usually aggregated; their primary form, an oblique rhombic prism.
humect: To moisten; to wet; to water.
humectate: To moisten; to wet; to water.
humectation: The act of moistening, wetting or watering.
humeral: Belonging to the shoulder; as the humeral artery.
humhum: A kind of plain, coarse India cloth, made of cotton.
humid: Moist; damp; containing sensible moisture; as a humid air or atmosphere.
Somewhat wet or watery; as humid earth.
humidity: Moisture; dampness; a moderate degree of wetness which is perceptible to the eye or touch, occasioned by the absorption of a fluid, or its adherence to the surface of a body. When a cloth has imbibed any fluid to such a degree that it can be felt, we call it humid; but when no humidity is perceptible, we say it is dry. Quicksilver communicates no humidity to our hands or clothes, for it does not adhere to them; but it will adhere to gold, tin and lead, and render them humid and soft to the touch.
Moisture in the form of visible vapor, or perceptible in the air.
humiliate: To humble; to lower in condition; to depress; as humiliated slaves.
humiliation: The act of humbling; the state of being humbled.
Descent from an elevated state or rank to one that is low or humble. The former was a humiliation of deity; the latter, a humiliation of manhood.
The act of abasing pride; or the state of being reduced to lowliness of mind, meekness, penitence and submission. The doctrine he preached was humiliation and repentance.
humility: In ethics, freedom from pride and arrogance; humbleness of mind; a modest estimate of one’s own worth. In theology, humility consists in lowliness of mind; a deep sense of one’s own unworthiness in the sight of God, self-abasement, penitence for sin, and submission to the divine will. Before honor is humility. Proverbs 15:33. Serving the Lord with all humility of mind. Acts 20:79.
Act of submission. With these humilities they satisfied the young king.
humite: A mineral of a reddish brown color, and a shining luster; crystallized in octahedrons, much modified by truncation and bevelment. It is named from Sir Abm. Hume.
humming: Making a low buzzing or murmuring sound.
The sound of bees; a low murmuring sound.
humor: Moisture; but the word is chiefly used to express the moisture or fluids of animal bodies, as the humors of the eye. But more generally the word is used to express a fluid in its morbid or vitiated state. Hence, in popular speech, we often hear it said, the blood is full of humors. But the expression is not technical nor correct. Aqueous humor of the eye, a transparent fluid, occupying the space between the crystalline lens and the cornea, both before and behind the pupil. Crystalline humor or lens, a small transparent solid body, of a softish consistence, occupying a middle position in the eye, between the aqueous and vitreous humors, and directly behind the pupil. It is of a lenticular form, or with double convex surfaces, and is the principal instrument in refracting the rays of light, so as to form an image on the retina. Vitreous humor of the eye, a fluid contained in the minute cel
A disease of the skin; cutaneous eruptions.

Turn of mind; temper; disposition, or rather a peculiarity of disposition often temporary; so called because the temper of mind has been supposed to depend on the fluids of the body. Hence we say, good humor; melancholy humor; peevish humor. Such humors, when temporary, we call freaks, whimsThus a person characterized by good nature may have a fit of ill humor; and an ill natured person may have a fit of good humor. So we say, it was the humor of the man at the time; it was the humor of the multitude.

That quality of the imagination which gives to ideas a wild or fantastic turn, and tends to excite laughter or mirth by ludicrous images or representations. Humor is less poignant and brilliant than wit; hence it is always agreeable. Wit, directed against folly, often offends by its severity; humor makes a man ashamed of his follies, without exciting his resentment. Humor may be employed solely to raise mirth and render conversation pleasant, or it may contain a delicate kind of satire.
Petulance; peevishness; better expressed by ill humor. Is my friend all perfection? has he not humors to be endured?
humoral: Pertaining to or proceeding from the humors; as a humoral fever. Humoral pathology, that pathology, or doctrine of the nature of diseases, which attributes all morbid phenomena to the disordered condition of the fluids or humors.
humoring: Indulging a particular wish or propensity; favoring; contributing to aid by falling into a design or course.
humorist: One who conducts himself by his own inclination, or bent of mind; one who gratifies his own humor. The humorist is one that is greatly pleased or greatly displeased with little things; his actions seldom directed by the reason and nature of things.
One that indulges humor in speaking or writing; one who has a playful fancy or genius.
One who has odd conceits; also, a wag; a droll.
humorous: Containing humor; full of wild or fanciful images; adapted to excite laughter; jocular; as a humorous essay; a humorous story.
Having the power to speak or write in the style of humor; fanciful; playful; exciting laughter; as a humorous man or author.
Subject to be governed by humor or caprice; irregular; capricious; whimsical. I am known to be a humorous patrician. Rough as a storm, and humorous as the’ wind.
humorously: With a wild or grotesque combination of ideas; in a manner to excite laughter or mirth; pleasantly; jocosely. Addison describes humorously the manual exercise of ladies’ fans.
Capriciously; whimsically; in conformity with one’s humor. We resolve by halves, rashly and humorously.
humorousness: The state or quality of being humorous; oddness of conceit; jocularity.
humorsovme: Peevish; petulant; influenced by the humor of the moment.
hump: The protuberance formed by a crooked back; as a camel with one hump, or two humps.
hunch: A hump; a protuberance; as the hunch of a camel.
A lump; a thick piece; as a hunch of bread; a word in common vulgar use in New England.
A push or jerk with the fist or elbow.
To push with the elbow; to push or thrust with a sudden jerk.
To push out in a protuberance; to crook the back.
hundred: Denoting the product of ten multiplied by ten, or the number of ten times ten; as a hundred men.
A collection, body or sum, consisting of ten times ten individuals or units; the number 100.
A division or part of a county in England, supposed to have originally contained a hundred families, or a hundred warriors, or a hundred manors.
hundred-court: In England, a court held for all the inhabitants of a hundred.
hundreder: In England, a man who may be of a jury in any controversy respecting land within the hundred to which he belongs.
One having the juriscition of a hundred.
hungary-water: A distilled water prepared from the tops of flowers of rosemary; so called from a queen of Hungary, for whose use it as first made.
hunger: An uneasy sensation occasioned by the want of food; a craving of food by the stomach; craving appetite. Hunger is not merely want of food, for persons when sick, may abstain long from eating without hunger, or an appetite for food. Hunger therefore is the pain or uneasiness of the stomach of a healthy person, when too long destitute of food.
Any strong or eager desire. For hunger of my gold I die.
To feel the pain or uneasiness which is occasioned by long abstinence from food; to crave food.
To desire with great eagerness; to long for. Blessed are they that hunger and thirst after righteousness. Matthew 5:6.
hunger-bit: Pained, pinched or weakened by hunger.
hunger-bitten: Pained, pinched or weakened by hunger.
hungering: Feeling the uneasiness of want of food; desiring eagerly; longing for; craving.
hunger-starved: Starved with hunger; pinched by want of food.
hungred: Hungry; pinched by want of food.
hungrily: With keen appetite; voraciously. When on harsh acorns hungrily they fed.
hungry: Having a keen appetite; feeling pain or uneasiness from want of food. Eat only when you are hungry.
Lean; emaciated, as if reduced by hunger. Cassius has a lean and hungry look.
Not rich or fertile; poor; barren; requiring substances to enrich itself; as a hungry soil; a hungry gravel.
hunks: A covetous sordid man; a miser; a niggard.
huns: The Scythians who conquered Pannonia, and gave it its present name, Hungary.
hunt: To chase wild animals, particularly quadrupeds, for the purpose of catching them for food, or for the diversion of sportsmen; to pursue with hounds for taking, as game; as, to hunt stag or a hare.
To go in search of, for the purpose of shooting; as, to hunt wolves, bears, squirrels or partridges. This is the common use of the word in America. It includes fowling by shooting.
To pursue; to follow closely. Evil shall hunt the violent man to overthrow him. Psalm 140:11.

To use, direct or mange hounds in the chase. He hunts a pack of dogs. To hunt out or after, to seek; to search for. To hunt from, to pursue and drive out or away. To hunt down, to depress; to bear down by persecution or violence.
hunter: One who pursues wild animals with a view to take them, either for sport or for food.
A dog that scents game, or is employed in the chase.
A horse used in the chase.
hunting: Chasing for seizure; pursuing; seeking; searching.

The act or practice of pursuing wild animals, for catching or killing them. Hunting was originally practiced by men for the purpose of procuring food, as it still is by uncivilized nations. But among civilized men, it is practiced mostly for exercise or diversion, or for the destruction of noxious animals, as in America.
hunting-horn: A bugle; a horn used to cheer the hounds in pursuit of game.
hunting-seat: A temporary residence for the purpose of hunting.
huntress: A female that hunts, or follows the chase. Diana is called the huntress.
huntsman: One who hunts, or who practices hunting.
The servant whose office it is to manage the chase.
huntsmanship: The art or practice of hunting, or the qualifications of a hunter.
hurdle: A texture of twigs, osiers or sticks; a crate of various forms, according to its destination. The English give this name to a sled or crate on which criminals are drawn to the place of execution. In this sense, it is not used in America.

In fortification, a collection of twigs or sticks interwoven closely and sustained by long stakes. It is made in the figure of a long square, five or six feet by three and a half. Hurdles serve to render works firm, or to cover traverses and lodgments for the defense of workmen against fire-works or stones.
In husbandry, a frame of split timber or sticks wattled together, serving for gates, inclosures.
hurds: The coarse part of flax or hemp.
hurdy-gurdy: An instrument of music, said to be used in the streets of London.
hurl: To throw with violence; to drive with great force; as, to hurl a stone. And hurl them headlong to their fleet and main.
To utter with vehemence; as, to hurl out vows.
To play at a kind of game.
The act of throwing with violence.
hurlbat: A whirl-bat; an old kind of weapon.
hurlbone: In a horse, a bone near the middle of the buttock.
hurler: One who hurls, or who plays at hurling.
hurling: Throwing with force; playing at hurling.
hurricane: A most violent storm of wind, occurring often in the West Indies, and sometimes in higher northern latitudes, and on the coast of the United States, as far north as New England. A hurricane is distinguished from every other kind of tempest by the extreme violence of the wind, and by its sudden changes; the wind often veering suddenly several points, sometimes a quarter of the circle and even more.
hurried: Hastened; urged or impelled to rapid motion or vigorous action.
hurrier: One who hurries, urges or impels.
hurry: To hasten; to impel to greater speed; to drive or press forward with more rapidity; to urge to act or proceed with more celerity; as, to hurry the workmen or the work. Our business hurries us. The weather is hot and the load heavy; we cannot safely hurry the horses.
To drive or impel with violence. Impetuous lust hurries him on to satisfy the cravings of it.

To urge or drive with precipitation and confusion; for confusion is often caused by hurry. And wild amazement hurries up and downN . The little number of your doubtful friends. To hurry away, to drive or carry away in haste.
To move or act with haste; to proceed with celerity or precipitation. The business is urgent; let us hurry.
A driving or pressing forward in motion or business.
hurrying: Driving or urging to greater speed; precipitating.
hurst: A wood or grove; a word found in many names, as in Hazlehurst.
hurt: To bruise; to give pain by a contusion, pressure, or any violence to the body. We hurt the body by a severe blow, or by tight clothes, and the feet by fetters. Psalm 105:18.
To wound; to injure or impair the sound state of the body, as by incision or fracture.
To harm; to damage; to injure by occasioning loss. We hurt a man by destroying his property.
To injure by diminution; to impair. A man hurts his estate by extravagance.
hurter: One who hurts or does harm.
hurters: Pieces of wood at the lower end of a platform, to prevent the wheels of gun-carriages from injuring the parapet.
hurtful: Injurious; mischievous; occasioning loss or destruction; tending to impair or destroy. Negligence is hurtful to property; intemperance is hurtful to health.
hurtfulness: Injuriousness; tendency to occasion loss or destruction; mischievousness.
hurtle: To clash or run against; to jostle; to skirmish; to meet in shock and encounter; to wheel suddenly.
To move with violence or impetuosity.
hurtless: Harmless; innocent; doing no injury; innoxious; as hurtless blows.
husband: A man contracted or joined to a woman by marriage. A man to whom a woman is betrothed, as well as one actually united by marriage, is called a husband. Leviticus 19:20; Deuteronomy 22:22-
In seaman’s language, the owner of a ship who manages its concerns in person.
The male of animals of a lower order.
An economist; a good manager; a man who knows and practices the methods of frugality and profit. In this sense, the word is modified by an epithet; as a good husband; a bad husband.
husbanded: Used or managed with economy; well managed.
husbandman: A farmer; a cultivator or tiller of the ground; one who labors in tillage. In America, where men generally own the land on which they labor, the proprietor of a farm is also a laborer or husbandman; but the word includes the lessee and the owner.
husbandry: The business of a farmer, comprehending agriculture or tillage of the ground, the raising, managing and fattening of cattle and other domestic animals, the management of the dairy and whatever the land produces.
Frugality; domestic economy; good management; thrift. But in this sense we generally prefix good; as good husbandry.
hush: Silent; still; quiet; as, they are hush as death. This adjective never precedes the noun which it qualifies, except in the compound
To still; to silence; to calm; to make quiet; to repress noise; as, to hush the noisy crown; the winds were hushed. My tongue shall hush again this storm of war.
To appease; to allay; to calm, as commotion or agitation. Wilt thou then . Hush my cares?
To be still; to be silent.
imperative of the verb, used as an exclamation, be still; be silent or quiet; make no noise. To hush up, to suppress; to keep concealed. This matter is hushed up.
hushmoney: A bribe to secure silence; money paid to hinder information, or disclosure of facts.
husk: The external covering of certain fruits or seeds of plants. It is the calyx of the flower or glume of corn and grasses, formed of valves embracing the seed. The husks of the small grains, when separated, are called chaff; but in America we apply the word chiefly to the covering of the ears or seeds of maiz, which is never denominated chaff. It is sometimes used in England for the rind, skin or hull of seeds.
To strip off the external integument or covering of the fruits or seeds of plants; as, to husk maiz.
huskiness: The state of being dry and rough, like a husk.
husking: The act of stripping off husks. In New England, the practice of farmers it to invite their neighbors to assist them in stripping their maiz, in autumnal evenings, and this is called a husking.
husky: Abounding with husks; consisting of husks.
huso: A fish of the genus Accipenser, whose mouth is in the under part of the head; the body is naked, or without prickles or protuberances. It grows to the length of twenty four feet, and its skin is so tough that it is used for ropes in drawing wheel-carriages. It inhabits the Danube and the rivers of Russia, and of its sounds is made isinglass.
hussar: s as z. A mounted soldier of horseman, in German cavalry. The hussars are the national cavalry of Hungary and Croatia. Their regimentals are a fur cap adorned with a feather, a doublet, a pair of breeches to which the stockings are fastened, and a pair of red or yellow boots. Their arms are a saber, a carbine and pistols. Hussars now form a part of the French and English cavalry.
hussite: A follower of John Huss, the Bohemian reformer.
hussy: A bad or worthless woman. It is used also ludicrously in slight disapprobation or contempt. Go, hussy.
hustings: A court held in Guildhall, in London, before the lord mayor and aldermen of the city; the supreme court or council of the city. In this court are elected the aldermen and the four members of parliament.
The place where an election of a member of parliament is held.
hustle: hus’l. To shake together in confusion; to push or crowd.
huswife: A worthless woman; a bad manager.
A female economist; a thrifty woman.
To manage with economy and frugality.
huswifery: The business of managing the concerns of a family by a female; female management, good or bad.
hut: A small house, hovel or cabin; a mean lodge or dwelling; a cottage. It is particularly applied to log-houses erected for troops in winter.
To place in huts, as troops encamped in winter quarters.
To take lodgings in huts. The troops hutted for the winter.
hutting: Placing in huts; taking lodgings in huts.
hutch: HUxXTo fish for pike with hooks and lines fastened to floating bladders.
A chest or box; a corn chest or bin; a case for rabbits.
huzza: A shout of joy; a foreign word used in writing only, and most preposterously, as it is never used in practice. The word used in our native word hoora, or hooraw.
To utter a loud shout of joy, or an acclamation in joy or praise.
To receive or attend with shouts of joy.
hyacinth: In botany, a genus of plants, of several species, and a great number of varieties. The oriental hyacinth has a large, purplish, bulbous root, from which spring several narrow erect leaves; the flower stalk is upright and succulent, and adorned with many bell- shaped flowers, united in a large pyramidical spike, of different colors in the varieties.

In mineralogy, a mineral, a variety of zircon, whose crystals, when distinct, have the form of a four-sided prism, terminated by four rhombic planes, which stand on the lateral edges. Its structure is foliated; its luster, strong; its fractureIts prevailing color is a hyacinth red, in which the red is more or less tinged with yellow or brown. It is sometimes transparent, and sometimes only translucent. Hyacinth is a subspecies of pyramidical zircon.
hyacinthine: Made of hyacinth; consisting of hyacinth; resembling hyacinth.
hyads: In astronomy, a cluster of seven stars in the Bull’s head, supposed by the ancients to bring rain.
hyaline: Glassy; resembling glass; consisting of glass.
hyalite: Muller's glass. It consists chiefly of silex, and is white, sometimes with a shade of yellow, blue or green.
hybrid: A mongrel or mule; an animal or plant, produced from the mixture of two species.
Mongrel; produced from the mixture of two species.
hybridous: Mongrel; produced from the mixture of two species.
hydage: In law, a tax on lands, at a certain rate by the hyde.
hydatid: A little transparent vesicle or bladder filled with water, on any part of the body, as in dropsy. Hydatids are certain spherical bodies, found occasionally in man, as well as in other animals, lodged in or adhering to the different viscera. Some of them, at least, are considered as possessing an independent vitality, and as constituting a distinct animal, allied to the toenia or tape-worm. They consist of a head, neck, and vesicular body filled with a transparent fluid.
hydatis: A little transparent vesicle or bladder filled with water, on any part of the body, as in dropsy. Hydatids are certain spherical bodies, found occasionally in man, as well as in other animals, lodged in or adhering to the different viscera. Some of them, at least, are considered as possessing an independent vitality, and as constituting a distinct animal, allied to the toenia or tape-worm. They consist of a head, neck, and vesicular body filled with a transparent fluid.
hydra: A water serpent. In fabulous history, a serpent or monster in the lake or marsh of Lerna, in Peloponnesus, represented as having many heads, one of which, being cut off, was immediately succeeded by another, unless the wound was cauterized. Hercules killed this monster by applying firebrands to the wounds, as he cut off the heads. Hence we give the name to a multitude of evils, or to a cause of multifarious evils.
A technical name of a genus of Zoophytes, called polypus, or polypuses.
hydracid: An acid formed by the union of hydrogen with a substance without oxygen.
hydragogue: hy’dragog. A medicine that occasions a discharge of watery humors; a name that implies a supposition that every purgative has the quality of evacuating a particular humor. But in general, the stronger cathartics are hydragogues.
hydrangea: A plant which grows in the water, and bears a beautiful flower. Its capsule has been compared to a cup.
hydrant: A pipe or machine with suitable valves and a spout, by which water is raised and discharged from the main conduit of an aqueduct.
hydrate: In chimistry, a compound, indefinite proportions, of a metallic oxyd with water. A hydrate is a substance which has formed so intimate a union with water as to solidify it, and render it a component part. Slaked lime is a hydrate of lime.
hydraulic: Relating to the conveyance of water through pipes.
Transmitting water through pipes; as a hydraulic engine. Hydraulic lime, a species of lime that hardens in water; used for cementing under water.
hydraulical: Relating to the conveyance of water through pipes.
Transmitting water through pipes; as a hydraulic engine. Hydraulic lime, a species of lime that hardens in water; used for cementing under water.
hydraulics: The science of the motion and force of fluids, and of the construction of all kinds of instruments and machines by which the force of fluids is applied to practical purposes; a branch of hydrostatic. Hydraulics is that branch of the science of hydrodynamics which treats of fluids considered as in motion.
hydrenterocele: A dropsy of the scrotum with rupture.
hydroidic: Denoting a peculiar acid or gaseous substance, produced by the combination of hydrogen and iodine.
hydriodate: A salt formed by the hydriodic acid, with a base.
hydrocarbonate: Carbureted hydrogen gas, or heavy inflammable air.
hydrocele: Any hernia proceeding from water; a watery tumor, particularly one in the scrotum. A dropsy of the scrotum.
hydrocephalus: Dropsy of the head; a preternatural distension of the head by a stagnation and extravasation of the lymph, either within or without the cranium.
hydrochlorate: A compound of hydrochloric acid and a base; a muriate.
hydrochloric: Hydrochloric acid is muriatic acid gas, a compound of chlorine and hydrogen gas.
hydrocyanic: The hydrocyanic acid is the same as the prussic acid.
hydrodynamic: Pertaining to the force or pressure of water.
hydrodynamics: That branch of natural philosophy which treat of the phenomena of water and other fluids, whether in motion or at rest; of their equilibrium, motion, cohesion, pressure, resistanceIt comprehends both hydrostatics and hydraulics.
hydrofluate: A compound of hydrofluoric acid and a base.
hydrofluoric: Consisting of fluorin and hydrogen. The hydrofluoric acid is obtained by distilling a mixture of one part of the purest fluor spar in fine powder, with two of sulphuric acid.
hydrogen: In Chimistry, a gas which constitutes one of the elements of water, of which it is said by Lavoisier to form fifteen parts in a hundred; but according to Berzelius and Dulong, hydrogen gas is 11.1 parts in a hundred, and oxygen 88.9. Hydrogen gas is an aeriform fluid, the lightest body known, and though extremely inflammable itself, it extinguishes burning bodies, and is fatal to animal life. Its specific gravity is 0.0694, that of air being 1.00. In consequence of its extreme lightness, it is employed for filling air balloons.
hydrogenate: To combine hydrogen with any thing.
hydrographer: One who draws maps of the sea, lakes or other waters, with the adjacent shores; one who describes the sea or other waters.
hydrographic: Relating to or containing a description of the sea, sea coast, isles, shoals, depth of wateror of a lake.
hydrographical: Relating to or containing a description of the sea, sea coast, isles, shoals, depth of wateror of a lake.
hydrography: The art of measuring and describing the sea, lakes, rivers and other waters; or the art of forming charts, exhibiting a representation of the sea coast, gulfs, bays, isles, promontories, channels, soundings
hydroguret: A compound of hydrogen with a base. Hydroguret is now scarcely used, except to give the derivative hydrogureted.
hydrogureted: Denoting a compound of hydrogen with a base.
hydrolite: A mineral whose crystals are described as six sided prisms, terminated by low six sided pyramids, with truncated summits.
hydrology: The science of water, its properties and phenomena.
hydromancy: A method of divination or prediction of events by water; invested, according to Varro, by the Persians, and practiced by the Romans.
hydromel: A liquor consisting of honey diluted in water. Before fermentation, it is called simple hydromel; after fermentation, it is called vinous hydromel or mead.
hydrometer: An instrument to measure the gravity, density, velocity, forceof water and other fluids, and the strength of spirituous liquors.
hydrometric: Pertaining to a hydrometer, or to the measurement of the gravityof fluids.
hydrometrical: Pertaining to a hydrometer, or to the measurement of the gravityof fluids.
hydrometry: The art of measuring, or the mensuration of the gravity, density, velocity, forceof fluids, and the strength of rectified spirits.
hydro-oxyd: A metallic oxyd combined with water; a metallic hydrate.
hydrophane: In mineralogy, a variety of opal made transparent by immersion in water.
hydrophanous: Made transparent by immersion in water.
hydrophobia: A preternatural dread of water; a symptom of canine madness, or the disease itself, which is thus denominated. This dread of water sometimes takes place in violent inflammations to the stomach, and in hysteric fits.
hydrophoby: A preternatural dread of water; a symptom of canine madness, or the disease itself, which is thus denominated. This dread of water sometimes takes place in violent inflammations to the stomach, and in hysteric fits.
hydrophobic: Pertaining to a dread of water, or canine madness.
hydropic: Containing water; caused by extravasated water; as a hydropic swelling.
Resembling dropsy. Every lust is a kind of hydropic distemper, and the more we drink the more we shall thirst.
hydropical: Containing water; caused by extravasated water; as a hydropic swelling.
Resembling dropsy. Every lust is a kind of hydropic distemper, and the more we drink the more we shall thirst.
hydropneumatic: An epithet given to a vessel of water, with other apparatus for chimical experiments.
hydroscope: A kind of water clock, or instrument used anciently for measuring time, consisting of a cylindrical tube, conical at the bottom, perforated at the vertex, and the whole tube graduated.
hydrostatic: Relating to the science of weighing fluids, or hydrostatics.
hydrostatical: Relating to the science of weighing fluids, or hydrostatics.
hydrostatically: According to hydrostatics, or to hydrostatic principles.
hydrostatics: The science which treats of the weight, motion, and equilibriums of fluids, or of the specific gravity and other properties of fluids, particularly of water. Hydrostatics is that branch of the science of hydrodynamics which treats of the properties of fluids at rest.
hydrosulphuret: A combination of sulphureted hydrogen with an earth, alkali or metallic oxyd.
hydrosulphureted: Combined with sulphureted hydrogen. Hydrosulphuric acid, is called also hydrothionic acid, or sulphureted hydrogen.
hydrotic: A medicine that purges off water or phlegm.
hydroxanthate: In chimistry, a compound of hydroxanthic acid with a base.
hydroxanthic: A term used to denote a new acid, formed by the action of alkalies on the bisulphuret of carbon. It is called also carbo-sulphuric acid.
hydruret: A combination of hydrogen with sulphur, or of sulphur and sulphureted hydrogen.
hydrus: A water snake; also, a constellation of the southern hemisphere.
hyemal: Belonging to winter; done in winter.

hyena: A quadruped of the genus Canis, having small naked ears, four toes on each foot, a straight jointed tail, and erect hair on the neck; an inhabitant of Asiatic Turkey, Syria, Persia and Barbary. hygrometry: The act or art of measuring the moisture of the air.

hylozoic: One who holds matter to be animated.

hymenoptera: In entomology, the hymenopters are an order of insects, having four membranous wings, and the tail of the female mostly armed with a sting.

hym. To praise in song; to worship by singing hymns.

hym. To sing in praise or adoration.
hyosciama: A new vegetable alkali, extracted from the Hyoscyamus nigra, or henbane.
hyp: To make melancholy; to depress the spirits.

hyperbola: In conic sections and geometry, a curve formed by cutting a cone in a direction parallel to its axis. A section of a cone, when the cutting plane makes a greater angle with the base than the side of the cone makes. The latter definition is the most correct.
hyperbole: hyper’boly. In rhetoric, a figure of speech which expresses much more or less than the truth, or which represents things much greater or less, better or worse than they really are. An object uncommon in size, either great or small, strikes us with surprise, and this emotion produces a momentary conviction that the object is greater or less than it is in reality. The same effect attends figurative grandeur or littleness; and hence the use of the hyperbole, which expresses this momentary conviction. The following are instances of the use of this figure. He was owner of a piece of ground not larger than a Lacedemonian letter. If a man can number the dust of the earth, then shall thy seed also be numbered. Genesis 13:16. lpse arduus, alta que pulsat Sidera. He was so gaunt, the case of a flagellet was a mansion for him.
hyperbolic: Belonging to the hyperbola; having the nature of the hyperbola.

hyperbolical: Belonging to the hyperbola; having the nature of the hyperbola.

hypercarbureted: Supercarbureted; having the largest proportion of carbon.
hypercatalectic: A hypercatalectic verse, in Greek and Latin poetry, is a verse which has a syllable or two beyond the regular and just measure.

hypercritical: Over critical; critical beyond use or reason; animadverting on faults with unjust severity; as a hypercritical reader.

hyperdulia: Super-service in the Romish church, performed to the virgin Mary.
hypermeter: Anything greater than the ordinary standard of measure. A verse is called a hypermeter, when it contains a syllable more than the ordinary measure. hyperoxyd: Acute to excess, as a crystal.
hyperoxymuriatic: The hyperoxymuriatic acid is the chloric acid.

hypersthene: A mineral, Labrador hornblend, or schillerspar. Its color is between grayish and greenish black, but nearly copper-red on the cleavage. So named from its difficult frangibility.

hypobole: hypob’oly. In rhetoric, a figure in which several things are mentioned that seem to make against the argument or in favor of the opposite side, and each of them is refuted in order.
